attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of a painful lump in his right axilla. The pain has been gradually worsening and he now finds it difficult to move his arm. He has had two similar episodes in the past year that resolved with antibiotics. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a gym instructor.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 4 cm tender, erythematous, fluctuant mass in his right axilla with surrounding cellulitis. There is no lymphadenopathy or discharge.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of a furuncle (boil or bacterial folliculitis), which is a deep infection of the hair follicle usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus. The presentation of a tender, fluctuant mass with surrounding erythema in a hair-bearing area is characteristic. The recurrent nature and occupation (frequent sweating) are predisposing factors, and patients may be carriers of Staphylococcus aureus which may also predispose them to developing recurrent boils.

Furuncle typically begins as a tender nodule that becomes fluctuant over several days as pus accumulates. They commonly occur in areas subject to friction and sweating such as the axilla, groin, and neck. Treatment may consist of warm compresses for small uncomplicated furuncles, or oral antibiotics and incision and drainage if there is spreading cellulitis or systemic symptoms. Community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (CA-MRSA) is an important consideration in recurrent abscesses and skin swabs should be taken if infections are recurrent.

Hidradenitis suppurativa typically presents with multiple recurring inflammatory nodules in intertriginous areas, often with sinus tract formation and scarring. While it can occur in the axilla, the absence of chronic scarring or multiple sites makes this diagnosis less likely.

Lymphadenitis would present as a tender, enlarged lymph node, but would not typically be fluctuant unless an abscess had formed. The location and characteristics are more consistent with a furuncle.

Lipoma is a benign soft tissue tumour that is typically non-tender, mobile, and rubbery in consistency. It would not present with acute pain, erythema, or fluctuance.

Sebaceous cyst is a benign cyst filled with keratin debris. While it can become infected, it typically presents as a long-standing, non-tender bump that has been present for months to years before becoming problematic.
learningPoints: Skin abscesses typically present with a tender, fluctuant mass with surrounding erythema and warmth.
references: Oakley A, Ngan V, Gomez J. Boil. DermNet NZ. 2016. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/boil

Spurling G, Askew D, King D, Mitchell G. Bacterial skin infections: An Observational study. Australian Family Physician. 2009: 38 (7). Available: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/2a153546-6ce9-467b-9a50-938682cf21ca/Bacterial-skin-infections.aspx
